**Harpenden** ([/ˈhɑːrpəndən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a town and [civil parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") in the [City and District of St Albans](/wiki/City_and_District_of_St_Albans "City and District of St Albans") in the county of [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire"), England. The population of the built-up area was 30,674 in the 2021 census, while the population of the civil parish was 31,128.[[3]](#cite_note-nomis1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-nomis2-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Harpenden is a [commuter town](/wiki/Commuter_town "Commuter town"), with a direct rail connection to [Central London](/wiki/Central_London "Central London").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Harpenden_Town_Centre.jpg/330px-Harpenden_Town_Centre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harpenden_Town_Centre.jpg)

The Harpenden coat of arms sign before entering the town centre

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Church_Green_in_Harpenden.jpg/330px-Church_Green_in_Harpenden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Church_Green_in_Harpenden.jpg)

Church Green in Harpenden Town Centre

There is evidence of pre-Roman [Belgic](/wiki/Belgae "Belgae") farmers in the area. In 1867, several items were found, including a bronze [escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)"), rams-head shaped mounts, and a bronze bowl.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

There are [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") remains in the land around Harpenden, such as the site of a mausoleum in the park at Rothamsted.[[7]](#cite_note-7) A tumulus near the river Lea was opened in the 1820s, and it contained a stone sarcophagus of Romano-Celtic origin. Five objects dating from around 150 AD were inside, including a glass jug with a Mediterranean stamp and [samian ware](/wiki/Samian_ware "Samian ware") dishes used for [libations](/wiki/Libation "Libation").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The name Harpenden probably derives from the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *hearpedenu,* meaning '[harp](/wiki/Harp "Harp") valley'. Another theory suggests that the name perhaps derives from *herepæðdenu* meaning 'military road valley'. This theory is considered because Harpenden lies on the ancient [St Albans](/wiki/St_Albans "St Albans") - [Luton](/wiki/Luton "Luton") road.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Up to the 13th century, the area of the parish consisted of woodland with small hamlets and single farmsteads around cleared areas called "End" or "Green"; today, there are 19 Ends and 18 Greens in the areas of Harpenden and Wheathampstead parishes.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Harpenden village grew out of [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey")'s gradual clearing of woodland for farming and settlement within its Wheathampstead manor, granted by [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor") in 1060. The first reference to a parish church was in 1221 (when it was referred to as Harpendene) so it is inferred that the village grew up around then. The [church of St Nicholas](/wiki/St_Nicholas_Church,_Harpenden "St Nicholas Church, Harpenden") is the oldest church in the town, originally built as a [Chapel of ease](/wiki/Chapel_of_ease "Chapel of ease") in 1217.

Just beyond the southern edge of the town lies [Nomansland Common](/wiki/Nomansland_Common "Nomansland Common") (sometimes simply called "No Man's Land") upon which part of the [Second Battle of St Albans](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_St_Albans "Second Battle of St Albans") was fought during the [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses"). Nomansland Common also saw the first annually contested [steeplechase](/wiki/Steeplechase_(horse_racing) "Steeplechase (horse racing)") in England, in 1830, when it was organised by [Thomas Coleman](/wiki/Thomas_Coleman "Thomas Coleman"), and the last fight of 19th-century bare-knuckle fighter [Simon Byrne](/wiki/Simon_Byrne "Simon Byrne"). It was also the haunt of the highwaywoman [Lady Katherine Ferrers](/wiki/Lady_Katherine_Ferrers "Lady Katherine Ferrers"), better known as the "Wicked Lady".

A widespread but now little-known industry of Harpenden was [straw-weaving](/wiki/Straw-weaving "Straw-weaving"), a trade mainly carried out by women in the 19th century. A good straw weaver could make as much as a field labourer. The straw plaits were taken to specialist markets in [St Albans](/wiki/St_Albans "St Albans") or [Luton](/wiki/Luton "Luton") and bought by dealers to be converted into straw items such as boaters and other hats or bonnets.

The arrival of the railway system from 1860 and the sale of farms for residential development after 1880 radically changed Harpenden's surroundings. First the [Dunstable Branch](/wiki/Dunstable_Branch "Dunstable Branch") of the [Great Northern Railway](/wiki/Great_Northern_Railway_(Great_Britain) "Great Northern Railway (Great Britain)") passed through the Batford area, with a station later named [Harpenden East railway station](/wiki/Harpenden_East_railway_station "Harpenden East railway station") (this line is now closed and forms a cycle track). The [Midland Railway](/wiki/Midland_Railway "Midland Railway") main line was built in 1868 with a station near the main village, which still exists today, and the listed [Southdown Road Skew Bridge](/wiki/Southdown_Road_Skew_Bridge "Southdown Road Skew Bridge") nearby.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [Harpenden and Hemel Hempstead Railway](/wiki/Harpenden_and_Hemel_Hempstead_Railway "Harpenden and Hemel Hempstead Railway"), known locally as the Nicky Line, was opened in 1877.

Between 1848 and 1914, the common was a regular venue for [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing"). In his *History of Hertfordshire* in 1879, [John Edwin Cussans](/wiki/John_Edwin_Cussans "John Edwin Cussans") commented "Notwithstanding that these meetings are under the most unexceptional patronage as regards the Stewards, yet for two days in the year all the London pickpockets, sharpers and blackguards who happen to be out of gaol are permitted to make Harpenden their own and to make travelling in a first-class carriage on the [Midland Railway](/wiki/Midland_Railway "Midland Railway") a danger to men and an impossibility to ladies." Golf has been played on the Common since 1894 and it was at that time Harpenden Golf Club was set up by a group of Harpenden people with the help and a financial contribution of 5 pounds from Sir [John Bennet Lawes](/wiki/John_Bennet_Lawes "John Bennet Lawes") of [Rothamsted Manor](/wiki/Rothamsted_Manor "Rothamsted Manor"). The club moved to a new course at Hammonds End in 1931, at which time [Harpenden Common Golf Club](/wiki/Harpenden_Common_Golf_Club "Harpenden Common Golf Club") was formed by those who wanted to remain at the Common. In 1932 Bamville Cricket club was formed and shares part of the Common with the Golfers.

Harpenden is the home of [Rothamsted Manor](/wiki/Rothamsted_Manor "Rothamsted Manor") and Rothamsted Research (formerly [Rothamsted Experimental Station](/wiki/Rothamsted_Experimental_Station "Rothamsted Experimental Station") and later the Institute of Arable Crops Research), a leading centre for agricultural research. In front of its main building, which faces the common, is a stone, erected in 1893, commemorating 50 years of experiments by Sir [John Bennet Lawes](/wiki/John_Bennet_Lawes "John Bennet Lawes") and [Joseph Henry Gilbert](/wiki/Joseph_Henry_Gilbert "Joseph Henry Gilbert").

Lawes inherited the family estate at Rothamsted in 1834. Acknowledged as "the father of agricultural science", his early field experiments on Hertfordshire farms led him to patent a phosphate fertiliser, the sales of which enriched him immensely. With the proceeds, he established the experimental station, building laboratories in the 1850s. The station continued the development of the artificial fertilisers on which most modern farmers now depend. Some of the long-term 'classical field experiments' begun by Lawes and Gilbert remain in place to this day (such as Broadbalk) representing a unique resource for agricultural and environmental research.

In 1913 the [National Children's Home](/wiki/National_Children%27s_Home "National Children's Home") moved to Harpenden with a large site Highfield Oval which was home to over 200 children. The site featured a print works, a carpenters' and joiners' shop, a bootmakers shop and a farm where boys undertook apprenticeships. Girls were mainly trained in domestic service with some being trained in sewing and office work.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The children lived in a "family" of 8-10 children each run by a sister or house mother. The chapel was gift from [Joseph Rank](/wiki/Joseph_Rank "Joseph Rank") and was built in 1928.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The home was run on site until 1985.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The site is now the head office of [Youth with a Mission](/wiki/Youth_with_a_Mission "Youth with a Mission") an international Christian missionary organization.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The Harpenden Growth Study, one of the earliest longitudinal tests, was overseen by [James Mourilyan Tanner](/wiki/James_Mourilyan_Tanner "James Mourilyan Tanner") and monitored the development of many of the children over a number of years.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

During the Second World War, Harpenden was used to [evacuate](/wiki/Emergency_evacuation "Emergency evacuation") children from heavily bombed London. However, Harpenden was not totally confident in its safety, as evidenced by the now decaying Bowers Parade air raid shelters,[[17]](#cite_note-17) soon to be secured for the future. It has been suggested both that it be used for educational and emergency training purposes.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The Harpenden and District Local History Society[[19]](#cite_note-19) has a collection of local material and archives which can be consulted, and holds regular meetings on topics of historical interest.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

There are two [civil parishes](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish"): Harpenden and [Harpenden Rural](/wiki/Harpenden_Rural "Harpenden Rural"). The town straddles two valleys; a dry valley to the south-west containing the town centre and Harpenden Common, and the valley of the [River Lea](/wiki/River_Lea "River Lea") to the north-east, containing the Batford area.

As Harpenden is located in Hertfordshire, just outside London, it is an area of extremely high property costs. Land Registry data suggests that the average house price in Harpenden in the first quarter of 2006 was £500,902, compared to £287,277 for the St Albans district generally and £183,598 nationally. The data also indicates that an unusually high proportion of houses in Harpenden are owner occupied (81.4%, as opposed to 69.6% in the district generally and 66.2% nationally).[[20]](#cite_note-20) The average price of a detached house is over £900,000 as of January 2012.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Harpenden has a large number of its streets named after English literary figures on the east side of the town (an area known, unsurprisingly, as the *Poets' Corner*), including [Byron Road](/wiki/George_Byron,_6th_Baron_Byron "George Byron, 6th Baron Byron"), [Cowper Road](/wiki/William_Cowper "William Cowper"), [Kipling Way](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling"), [Milton Road](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton"), [Shakespeare Road](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"), [Spenser Road](/wiki/Edmund_Spenser "Edmund Spenser"), [Shelley Court](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley"), [Tennyson Road](/wiki/Alfred_Tennyson,_1st_Baron_Tennyson "Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson"), [Townsend Road](/wiki/Aurelian_Townshend "Aurelian Townshend"), [Masefield Road](/wiki/John_Masefield "John Masefield") and [Wordsworth Road](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth").

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Governance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Harpenden_Town_Hall.jpg/250px-Harpenden_Town_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harpenden_Town_Hall.jpg)

[Harpenden Town Hall](/wiki/Harpenden_Town_Hall "Harpenden Town Hall"), Leyton Road

There are three tiers of local government covering Harpenden, at [parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") (town), [district](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district") and [county level](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county"): Harpenden Town Council, [St Albans City and District Council](/wiki/St_Albans_City_and_District_Council "St Albans City and District Council") and [Hertfordshire County Council](/wiki/Hertfordshire_County_Council "Hertfordshire County Council"). The town council is based at [Harpenden Town Hall](/wiki/Harpenden_Town_Hall "Harpenden Town Hall") on Leyton Road.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Administrative history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Administrative history")]

Harpenden was anciently part of the parish of [Wheathampstead](/wiki/Wheathampstead "Wheathampstead"), in the [hundred](/wiki/Hundred_(division) "Hundred (division)") of [Dacorum](/wiki/List_of_hundreds_of_England "List of hundreds of England"). The chapelry of Harpenden was treated as a separate civil parish from an early date, having its own church wardens and parish registers from the sixteenth century. An order to create a separate parish of Harpenden was made in 1656, but does not appear to have been carried out. Harpenden was eventually made a separate ecclesiastical parish in 1859.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

From 1835 Harpenden was included within the St Albans [Poor Law Union](/wiki/Poor_Law_Union "Poor Law Union").[[27]](#cite_note-27) As such, Harpenden became part of the St Albans [Rural Sanitary District](/wiki/Rural_Sanitary_District "Rural Sanitary District") in 1872, which in turn became the [St Albans Rural District](/wiki/St_Albans_Rural_District "St Albans Rural District") under the [Local Government Act 1894](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1894 "Local Government Act 1894"). The 1894 Act also created parish councils. Harpenden Parish Council held its first meeting on 31 December 1894, with Captain Arthur Lydekker, a [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_party_(UK) "Conservative party (UK)"), being elected the first chairman.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) The parish council held its meetings at the Harpenden Institute at 12 Southdown Road (then called Wheathampstead Road).[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Shortly after the creation of St Albans Rural District, efforts began to make Harpenden an [urban district](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)"). It was decided that the whole parish of Harpenden was not suitable to become an urban district, with the west of the parish remaining largely rural. As such, Harpenden was split into two parishes on 15 April 1898, called Harpenden Urban and [Harpenden Rural](/wiki/Harpenden_Rural "Harpenden Rural"). The Harpenden Rural parish remained part of St Albans Rural District, whilst the Harpenden Urban parish became independent as Harpenden Urban District.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Arthur Lydekker, who had been chairman of the old Harpenden Parish Council, was appointed the first chairman of Harpenden Urban District Council.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Park_Hall_Harpenden.jpg/250px-Park_Hall_Harpenden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Park_Hall_Harpenden.jpg)

Park Hall, Harpenden

Until September 1899, Harpenden Urban District Council held its meetings at the Harpenden Institute, as the parish council had done.[[35]](#cite_note-35) On the opposite side of the Common was the British School on Leyton Road. The school had been built in 1850 but was vacated in 1897 when the school moved to new premises on Victoria Road. The council took a lease of the old British School from its owners, the Lawes family of [Rothamsted](/wiki/Rothamsted_Manor "Rothamsted Manor"), and the building was opened as the "Public Hall" in September 1899, acting as the council's meeting place and offices, as well as providing a public hall.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

In 1932, the council bought Harpenden Hall at 6 Southdown Road for £7,000 and converted it to act as offices and meeting place, moving into the building in early 1933.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) A new Public Hall was built in the former gardens of Harpenden Hall in 1938. The old public hall on Leyton Road became known as Park Hall.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The council was granted a coat of arms on 4 February 1949.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Harpenden Urban District Council was abolished under the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), becoming part of the new [district](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district") of St Albans on 1 April 1974. A [successor parish](/wiki/Successor_parish "Successor parish") was created for the former urban district, with its parish council taking the name Harpenden Town Council. The town council continued to be based at Harpenden Hall until 1996, when it moved to a new Town Hall built as a rear extension to the urban district council's former home of Park Hall.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Railway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Harpenden_Train_Station.JPG/250px-Harpenden_Train_Station.JPG)](/wiki/File:Harpenden_Train_Station.JPG)

The view north from platform 1 at Harpenden station

[Harpenden railway station](/wiki/Harpenden_railway_station "Harpenden railway station") is a stop on the [Thameslink](/wiki/Thameslink "Thameslink") route. [Govia Thameslink Railway](/wiki/Govia_Thameslink_Railway "Govia Thameslink Railway") operates frequent services between [Bedford](/wiki/Bedford_railway_station "Bedford railway station"), [Luton](/wiki/Luton_railway_station "Luton railway station"), [St Albans City](/wiki/St_Albans_City_railway_station "St Albans City railway station") and [London St Pancras](/wiki/London_St_Pancras "London St Pancras"). Southbound services continue through [Farringdon](/wiki/Farringdon_railway_station "Farringdon railway station") and [London Blackfriars](/wiki/London_Blackfriars_railway_station "London Blackfriars railway station"), then on to [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton_railway_station "Brighton railway station") (via [Gatwick Airport](/wiki/Gatwick_Airport_railway_station "Gatwick Airport railway station")), [Three Bridges](/wiki/Three_Bridges_railway_station "Three Bridges railway station") or [Rainham](/wiki/Rainham_railway_station_(Kent) "Rainham railway station (Kent)"). The rail link therefore gives direct access to [Luton Airport](/wiki/Luton_Airport "Luton Airport") (one stop north) and [Gatwick Airport](/wiki/Gatwick_Airport "Gatwick Airport"), taking approximately 1hr 10 m on a limited stops train.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The [Nickey Line](/wiki/Nickey_Line "Nickey Line") railway linked Harpenden, [Redbourn](/wiki/Redbourn "Redbourn") and [Hemel Hempstead](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead "Hemel Hempstead"); [Roundwood Halt](/wiki/Roundwood_Halt_railway_station "Roundwood Halt railway station") also served the north-west of the town. The line opened in 1877 and was closed in 1979; the trackbed was converted to a [shared-use path](/wiki/Shared-use_path "Shared-use path") in 1985, forming part of the [National Cycle Network](/wiki/National_Cycle_Network "National Cycle Network").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Rothamsted_Research.jpg/330px-Rothamsted_Research.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rothamsted_Research.jpg)

[Rothamsted Research](/wiki/Rothamsted_Research "Rothamsted Research") is the largest agricultural research centre in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")

Harpenden is a prosperous town. In an analysis of the average income tax paid by constituency, [Hitchin and Harpenden](/wiki/Hitchin_and_Harpenden "Hitchin and Harpenden") came tenth.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In a list of the most valuable commuter areas compiled by Savills Research, Harpenden came seventh. Good transport links to central London have been cited as a key factor.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[Rothamsted Research](/wiki/Rothamsted_Research "Rothamsted Research"), the largest agricultural research centre in the UK, is based in Harpenden. It was founded in 1843.

Harpenden has many shops commonly found in other English towns, with three central supermarkets ([Sainsbury's](/wiki/Sainsbury%27s "Sainsbury's"), [Marks and Spencer](/wiki/Marks_and_Spencer "Marks and Spencer"), and [Waitrose](/wiki/Waitrose "Waitrose")), multiple female clothes shops, charity shops, banks, estate agents and chemists. A good proportion of these are run by independent retailers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Cafes are also common in Harpenden, but with only three commercial chains ([Costa Coffee](/wiki/Costa_Coffee "Costa Coffee"), [Caffè Nero](/wiki/Caff%C3%A8_Nero "Caffè Nero"), and [Gail's](/wiki/Gail%27s "Gail's")); the rest are owned independently. ## Parks and commons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Parks and commons")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/View_of_the_18th_hole_at_Harpenden_Common_Golf_Club.jpg/250px-View_of_the_18th_hole_at_Harpenden_Common_Golf_Club.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_the_18th_hole_at_Harpenden_Common_Golf_Club.jpg)

A view of Harpenden Common Golf Club

A notable feature of Harpenden is its abundant parks and [commons](/wiki/Commons "Commons"). The central area of Harpenden is characterised by Church Green, Leyton Green and the High Street Greens, which give the town its provincial feel.

Just south of the town centre is Harpenden Common, stretching from the shops in the town centre for more than one mile (1.5 kilometres) to the south, encompassing a total of 238 acres (96 hectares). Today Harpenden Common hosts two cricket clubs: Harpenden Cricket Club, a Hertfordshire Premier League club that celebrated its 150 anniversary in 2013, and Bamville Cricket Club, which plays on Sundays on the golf course. There is also a football club, bridleways, ramblers' paths and Harpenden Common Golf Club, all contained in an area of natural beauty that was awarded a national [Green Flag Award](/wiki/Green_Flag_Award "Green Flag Award") in 2007. Harpenden Town Council is keen to help retain and maintain the environment and oversees habitat management, including bird and bat watching, and the maintenance/regeneration of gorse, fungi and other fauna and flora for the benefit of the people of Harpenden.

Since 1894, Harpenden Common Golf Club has traditionally maintained a large part of the common and today works closely with Harpenden Town Council and countryside management. This partnership has enabled the people of Harpenden to take full advantage of the common for all kinds of leisure activities.

In addition the town has large green public spaces available in [Rothamsted Park](/wiki/Rothamsted_Park "Rothamsted Park"), Batford Park, Kinsbourne Green, [Lydekker Park](/wiki/Lydekker_Park "Lydekker Park") and the [Nickey Line](/wiki/Nickey_Line "Nickey Line"), which bisects the town.

Just to the south of Harpenden is the large expanse of [Nomansland Common](/wiki/Nomansland_Common "Nomansland Common").

## Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Events")]

Harpenden Lions Highland Gathering continues a tradition that began in 1946.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The Gathering is held every July in Rothamsted Park and typically attracts 6,000 visitors. It claims to be the largest UK Highland Gathering outside of Scotland. It raises many thousands of pounds each year for charities nominated by Harpenden Lions Club who run the event.

An annual classic car show, Classics on the Common,[[52]](#cite_note-52) is held on the fourth Wednesday in July, attracting over 10,000 visitors and 1,300 cars. One of the biggest events of its type in Europe, it is a free event, run by Rotary in Harpenden, with any profits collected going to charity.

The annual HERTS 10K run in aid of Rennie Grove Hospice Care takes place on the second Sunday in October. The event attracts thousands of runners and walkers, making it one of the biggest 10k runs in Hertfordshire. The event starts and finishes at [Rothamsted Research](/wiki/Rothamsted_Research "Rothamsted Research").

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Culture")]

Harpenden Public Halls was a 410-seat live music and theatre venue in the town centre. It was replaced in 2021 with the newly built Eric Morecambe Centre in nearby [Rothamsted Park](/wiki/Rothamsted_Park "Rothamsted Park"), which is a 511-seat multi function space. The Public Halls site will be reused for housing.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Harpenden is also home to Musicale, a music school and music shop on the site of [St George's School](/wiki/St_George%27s_School,_Harpenden "St George's School, Harpenden") providing instrumental and vocal training to adults and children. It runs several orchestras and bands.

Other music groups based in Harpenden include The Lea Singers, Hardynge Choir, Magic Voices, Harpenden Choral Society, Music Makers, Harpenden Concert Band and Harpenden Musical Theatre Company.

As of September 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit), a new cultural hub Harpenden Arts Centre is set to launch as a fully accessible arts venue with a state of the art performance venue and rehearsal spaces for dance, music and drama.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

Harpenden is also home to the Harpenden Live festival, which takes place in July every two years to promote new musical artists in Harpenden, Hertfordshire, and beyond.

## Scouting and Guiding in Harpenden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harpenden&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Scouting and Guiding in Harpenden")]

There is [scouting](/wiki/Scouting_and_Guiding_in_the_United_Kingdom "Scouting and Guiding in the United Kingdom") in Harpenden. Scout groups fall under the heading of the "Harpenden and Wheathampstead District Scouts".

As well as taking part in a number of volunteer roles and marching in both the Remembrance and St George's Day parades, they are involved in the Harpenden and Wheathampstead District Scout [Gang Show](/wiki/Gang_Show "Gang Show"), an annual variety show. The Harpenden Gang Show is the world's longest continuously running Gang Show,[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) with a performance every year since 1949.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

There are also several Guide units in Harpenden. Guides take part in the Remembrance Sunday and St George's Day parades alongside the Scouts and has younger counterparts called Rainbows and Brownies.
